title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Elders dine on seized king crab HAUL Troopers confiscate 92 crab in Kotzebue; fisherman faces charges. By Joel Gay Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 10, 2002) Kotzebue elders enjoyed fresh king crab last week after Alaska state troopers seized the haul of a subsistence fisherman suspected of illegally selling his catch. Troopers said they received several tips that a juvenile related to the Kotzebue fisherman was selling king crab that the man had caught in Kotzebue Sound. After an investigation, troopers contacted the fisherman Friday and seized 92 crab. An estimated 20 other crab had already been sold, troopers said. Charges have been forwarded to the Kotzebue district attorney's office. The crab came from an experimental fishery to determine whether there are enough of the succu…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: July 10, 2002) Natural gas prices slip U.S. natural gas prices fell below $3 per million BTUs on Tuesday for the first time since March 14, closing at $2.99. The $3 price is considered a minimum that would need to be sustained to justify the multibillion-dollar cost of a project to bring North Slope gas to market. So far this year, prices have averaged $2.98, with a high of $3.81 on May 1 and a low of $1.98 on Jan. 29. The oil companies that own rights to the Slope's gas say the project doesn't make financial sense right now. Best Buy hiring for new store Best Buy has begun hiring for its South Anchorage store, which is expected to open in late summer or early fall. The store, at the old Safeway site in the Dimond Center mall, is expected to employ ab…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Puppy rescued from mine chute TRECIE: Dog fled fireworks, found after three-day search. The Associated Press TY: Wire (Published: July 10, 2002) Juneau -- Trecie, an 8-month-old malamute puppy, is back home after spending more than three days trapped in a chute deep in the city's only open-pit mine. Trecie's owners said the puppy fled the family's home July 3 because it panicked after hearing the sound of firecrackers being set off on Sandy Beach. She ran up the Treadwell Historic Mine Trail, a hiking path above Sandy Beach that leads to the Glory Hole. Richard Fraser and his wife, Londi, searched for several hours Thursday morning but could not find the puppy. They put up fliers describing her throughout the Douglas community. An acquaintance visited on Saturday and said he'd heard a …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska digest (Published: July 10, 2002) ANCHORAGE Transportation study group to host discussion on freight hauling Freight hauling is the topic of today's Brown Bag Lunch, the fifth in a series sponsored by the East Anchorage Study of Transportation, or EAST. The full title of the discussion is "Freight: If everything we use comes by truck, how are we doing?" EAST is a program of the Municipality of Anchorage, the state Department of Transportation and the engineering firm HDR Alaska. It is conducting an online questionnaire, at www.eastanchorage.net , regarding various issues related to mobility in the city's east half, including the controversial Bragaw Street extension. More information is also available from Carla Slaton Barker of HDR Alaska at 274-2000 or cslatonbToday's lunch ta…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Pair died in plane crash FOUND: Pilot Larry Erie and a passenger had been missing since July 4. By S.J. Komarnitsky Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 9, 2002) A longtime state Department of Fish and Game employee and a friend from Minnesota were found dead Monday along with the wreckage of their plane at 5,000 feet on a mountain in Anderson Pass near Mount McKinley. Larry Erie, 54, who lived near Wasilla, and a female friend, whose name was not being released because relatives had not been notified, had been missing since July 4, when they left for a day trip. A Fairbanks Civil Air Patrol squadron had been searching for the two since Saturday. Crews spotted the wreckage of Erie's red and white Super Cub about 1 p.m Monday in steep, rocky terrain about 25 miles northeast of Denali. …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Man admits killing girlfriend DEATH: Suspect says he felt relief after strangling his roommate. By Katie Pesznecker Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 9, 2002) Edward Alan Soeth told Anchorage police he spent 20 minutes enjoying the calm silence after he strangled girlfriend Cynthia Maughs on Friday. Soeth, 38, was arrested later that day and arraigned Saturday on charges of first-degree murder. Court records said Soeth felt himself "fill with rage" before attacking his live-in girlfriend. He told police he first strangled Maughs with his hands, then wrapped a ligature around her neck, possibly pushing one foot against her body for leverage. During interviews with police, Soeth discussed suicide and also joked around, comparing police officers to characters on the television show "N…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Young anglers hook big halibut 162 POUNDS: Tourists gather as boys play fish on local dock. The Associated Press (Published: June 18, 2002) Ketchikan -- A pair of 11-year-olds who have been fishing partners "for years" landed a 162-pound halibut off the Knudson Cove marine dock. The battle between Zach Hamilton and Greg Johnson and the big flatfish took more than two hours June 8 and drew a crowd of helpers and onlookers. Marina workers say they cannot recall anyone landing a larger fish from the dock. The boys began fishing in the afternoon and had put in two hours before they hooked the lunker. Zach was trying to catch bait. Greg was tending the poles when the fish hit. "I pretty much knew it was a halibut because it had the pole halfway in the water," Greg said. "I dove for the pole…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Plea deal nets man seven years KIDNAPPING: Pedro Disla receives probation, treatment in October 2000 crime. By Nicole Tsong Anchorage Daily News (Published: June 27, 2002) Pedro J. Disla, charged with kidnapping a girl in the Northway Mall parking lot in October 2000, was sentenced to seven years in prison Wednesday after agreeing to a plea deal. On Wednesday, Disla, 21, pleaded no contest in Anchorage Superior Court to felony charges of kidnapping, first-degree theft and first-degree eluding a peace officer. He also pleaded no contest to a charge of second-degree theft for stealing a bike at knifepoint from a 10-year-old boy in May 2000. Charges of first-degree robbery, second-degree sexual assault and third-degree assault were dropped as part of the plea bargain, said assistant distr…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Democrats push for veterans bill SPECIAL SESSION: GOP lawmakers say they prefer plan offered by Murkowski. By Mike Chambers The Associated Press (Published: June 27, 2002) Juneau -- Lawmakers neared the end of a turbulent third day of a special session with no agreement on two bills that would allow the Legislature to adjourn for the year. The day was capped with Democrats turning up the heat on majority Republicans to act on a veterans bill before they leave Juneau for the year. Democrat Gov. Tony Knowles called the Legislature into a special session to reauthorize the state's utility regulatory commission and to consider $2.6 million to allow veterans to stay in the Alaska Pioneers' Homes. The Legislature adjourned from a 121-day regular session seven days late without action on the …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Kmart drops all-night schedule 6 A.M.- MIDNIGHT: Slow business, shoplifters lead to new hours. By Sarana Schell Anchorage Daily News (Published: June 27, 2002) No more 24-7 Kmart for southern Alaska. After an eight-month trial, local managers said overnight lulls and security issues prompted their decision to cut store hours. The two Anchorage stores and the Juneau store are now open 6 a.m. to midnight, said Mike Wilson, store manager for Anchorage's Dimond Kmart. The Fairbanks store will remain open around the clock, Wilson said, because it does more business than the other stores during its brighter summer nights. The move to all-hours shopping was a corporate directive in the hopes of boosting profits, said Bob Newbry, manager of the East Anchorage store. The expanded hours also wer…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska digest (Published: July 7, 2002) ANCHORAGE Boyfriend of strangled woman arrested, charged with murder Police arrested the boyfriend of an Anchorage woman strangled Friday night and charged him with her murder. Cynthia L. Maughs, 44, was found dead in the bedroom of her Mountain View apartment just after 9 p.m. Friday, police said. Edward A. Soeth, 38, was charged with first-degree murder in her death. At 9:04 p.m., a friend of Soeth's called police dispatchers and said Soeth had told him he had killed his girlfriend and left her inside the couple's apartment. Patrol officers kicked in the door at 9:08 p.m. when no one answered, police said. At 9:14 p.m., Soeth walked up to an officer surrounding the apartment with crime scene tape and told the officer he lived there. Officers to…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Need a job? Alaska seafood industry is recruiting Local seafood industry woos Asian markets By Laine Welch TY: Freelance (Published: July 6, 2002) Kodiak -- The Alaska seafood industry got some encouraging news with the prospect of new markets in China and Hong Kong. Recently a delegation of 11 seafood buyers from that region toured the state to get a firsthand look at seafood processing operations and hook up with potential suppliers. The trip was organized by the Alaska Seafood Marketing Institute and funded by a federal grant. The trip actually began in Seattle, a main hub of Alaska's commercial fishing industry. Autumn Curry, ASMI's Asian marketing specialist, said that's where they began making business contacts. "We had sales meetings all day and everyone got a chance to do one-o…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Zoo bears go fishing for trout A GOOD SHOW: Oreo takes the lead after keepers stock pool. By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 7, 2002) To the delight of the crowd gathered, Oreo the Alaska Zoo grizzly bear, above, searches for rainbow trout Saturday in the bear pond of the special enclosure she shares with Ahpun the polar bear. The zoo got a permit from the Alaska Department of Fish and Game to stock the pond with 50 rainbow trout, small and fast enough to test the bears' hunting skills and possibly elude them and live in the pond. (Photo by Evan R. Steinhauser / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge The 50 rainbow trout darting through the bear pool were supposed to outrun Ahpun and Oreo. The two furry Alaska Zoo celebrities -- Ahpun the polar bear and Ore…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | High school football star killed in hit-run accident CRASH: Police seek two men whose pickup hit car, killing Aanooalii Filoialii. By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 7, 2002) Police on Saturday sought two men in a pickup that killed an 18-year-old college football hopeful when it ran a red light. Aanooalii Filoialii, an all-state defensive end at East High School last year, was killed in a hit-and-run accident at DeBarr Road and Airport Heights Drive around 12:45 a.m. Saturday, Anchorage police said. Filoialii was a passenger in a 2001 Dodge Neon hit by a 1977 Ford pickup, police said. The driver of the Neon, 17-year-old Karen Saena, was seriously injured and was hospitalized at Alaska Regional Hospital. Saena was a good friend of Filoialii's, Filoialii's father sai…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska digest (Published: June 23, 2002) ANCHORAGE Concourse B at airport evacuated because of suspected breach Security officials evacuated a portion of Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport on Saturday morning after a suspected security breach. Airport security Lt. Dave Tullis said security screeners at Concourse B called about 9:30 a.m. after they suspected a female passenger got into the concourse before being fully screened. When the passenger couldn't be found, Tullis said, the only solution was to empty the concourse and rescreen passengers. "If we can't ensure the concourse is sterile, our only recourse is to empty the area and start over," Tullis said. The concourse was cleared and ready to accept passengers within 20 minutes, Tullis said. But it took about 90 minutes of…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Federal board to limit Kuskokwim sportfishery SALMON: Panel reins in anglers during subsistence fishery closures. By Joel Gay Anchorage Daily News (Published: June 26, 2002) Fishermen on the Kuskokwim River are catching more king salmon than they have in years, but there still aren't enough of the big chinooks to satisfy demand. This spring, state and federal managers reduced subsistence fishing on the river to four days a week, saying the king run needed the additional protection. Biologists now say this year's return appears strong enough to meet subsistence needs and spawning goals. If runs continue to build, the restrictions will be lifted. In the meantime, some Kuskokwim-area subsistence fishermen asked the Federal Subsistence Board to shut down the area's sportfishery, saying the…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Energy bill is back on the table COMPROMISE: Fates of ANWR, natural gas pipeline won't be known for months. By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published: June 23, 2002) Washington -- It's been nearly a year since the House passed an energy bill allowing drilling in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge and almost two months since the Senate passed its ANWR-less version. House and Senate leaders have announced that they are finally ready to meet in a conference committee to iron out a compromise bill that might clear both chambers. The fate of two major Alaska items tied up in the bill -- ANWR and financial incentives for a North Slope natural gas pipeline -- likely won't be known for months, though. Rep. Billy Tauzin, the Louisiana Republican who will chair the conference, predicted the…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Controlled burn reignites MYSTERY CREEK: Unless it spreads, the blaze should help Kenai Peninsula moose. By Jon Little Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 4, 2002) A smoglike haze and smoky tang in the air may have irritated residents of Anchorage on Wednesday, but its source should benefit Kenai Peninsula moose in the long run. A prescribed burn smoldering since solstice weekend near Mystery Creek was whipped back to life by winds and dry air this week, producing the smoke. Fire crews laying hose on foot and a helicopter toting a water bucket fought to contain the spreading fire Wednesday as winds gusted to 20 mph. "We've been tested today (Wednesday) but we've been able to hold onto it so far," said Doug Newbould, fire management officer for the Kenai National Wildlife Refuge. Newb…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Mosquito veto bugs Larsen Bay LETDOWN: Knowles' cut hangs community out to the pests. The Associated Press (Published: July 2, 2002) Kodiak -- Gov. Tony Knowles' veto of mosquito-killing machines for Larsen Bay is bugging residents to no end. The idea for buying Mosquito Magnets came from city clerk Tammy Aga, who spotted the machines in a catalog on an airplane. "It's a big letdown for everyone," Aga said. "Until someone comes here and sees how bad it is, they have no idea." Larsen Bay is a community of more than 100 people on the northwest coast of Kodiak Island, 60 miles southwest of the city of Kodiak. Heat and no wind have made the bug problem especially bad in the past three days. Aga described it as a haze of bugs that must be seen to comprehend. Aga suggested that Knowles and o…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Voters will decide bond issues in fall PACKAGE: Eight new schools head list of construction projects. By Mike Chambers The Associated Press (Published: July 3, 2002) Alaska voters will go to the polls in November to decide the first general obligation bond package the state has had in more than 20 years. Voters will decide the fate of millions to construct eight new schools in Bush Alaska along with funding for other urban school projects. Voters will also decide on a $227 million road bond bill. Gov. Tony Knowles approved four bills on Tuesday during a ceremony at Clark Middle School in Anchorage, saying it would allow for nearly $800 million in construction projects to move forward. "These projects come at an opportune time, when interest rates are low," Knowles said. Alaska voters l…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Six cities say Exxon owes them $12 million CLAIM: Jury to decide on recovery of oil-spill related costs. By Nicole Tsong Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 3, 2002) Thirteen years after the tanker Exxon Valdez ran aground on Bligh Reef and spilled nearly 11 million gallons of crude oil into Prince William Sound, six cities claim Exxon Mobil Corp. stills owes them $12 million in costs associated with the spill. A trial over the claim is before a jury in Anchorage Superior Court. Winds, currents and tides carried the crude oil out of the Sound, around the outer coast of the Kenai Peninsula and southwest to Kodiak Island and beyond. The plaintiffs are Kodiak Island Borough, Seward, Cordova, Old Harbor, Larsen Bay and Port Lions. "People were working hard on the spill, and Exxon continu…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Knowles strikes back on budget PAYBACK: Governor signs after deleting millions in GOP projects. By Mike Chambers The Associated Press (Published: June 29, 2002) Juneau -- Democrat Gov. Tony Knowles signed into law a $2.4 billion budget that funds state government next fiscal year, but deleted millions in GOP projects. Knowles capped a legislative session filled with partisan rancor by axing about $23 million in projects Republicans sought for their districts. The governor chided lawmakers for cutting essential state services in the same year as funding such projects. "It makes a mockery of the cries for fiscal restraint," Knowles said. Alaska's fiscal straits were a pressing concern for the Legislature this year as the state faces a $1 billion budget deficit in future years. The state …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | McLeod enjoys turn Down Under BLUEGRASS: Local band performs at huge Australian festival. By Rose Cox Anchorage Daily News (Published: June 28, 2002) The world got a little smaller when members of local bluegrass band McLeod met a couple of folks from Australia while performing at Borders Books and Music one night. Visitors Pauline Selmes and Geoff Stewart told the band about a humongous music festival Down Under, then helped them apply to perform. McLeod was one of three U.S. acts invited to the National Folk Festival in Australia's capital, Canberra, in late March. The festival, a showcase primarily for Australian culture, included musicians, dancers, musical comedy groups and storytellers. One event, the World Poetry Debate, challenged participants to use prose, rhyme and rhythm to …

Frank Murkowski is back from Washington, D.C., this week, visiting family and campaigning. (Photo by Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) Jerry Hood of Teamsters Local 959 speaks at a press conference called by Sen. Frank Murkowski on Tuesday at the Port of Anchorage. Murkowski said he had a "major campaign announcement." (Photo by Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge U.S. Sen. Frank Murkowski said that he wants an Outside group running ads attacking the Knowles administration to end the television spots. "I'd like to see them stopped," Murkowski said Tuesday, referring to the issue advertis…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Rowing Revival 5-year-old Anchorage club growing, planning for future By Natalie Phillips Anchorage Daily News (Published: June 23, 2002) Greer Gehler, 19, rows with the women's masters. The club has members from age 12 through 60-plus. (Photo by Bill Roth / Anchorage Daily News) Rowing coach Sarah Riggs motors across Sand Lake while working with three eight-person boats and one four-person boat. "This is the best job I've ever had," says Riggs, 24. (Photo by Bill Roth / Anchorage Daily News) Jesica Shae, left, is one of eight rowers practicing on Sand Lake. Each rower has a 12-foot oar to command. (Photo by Bill Roth / Anchorage Daily News) Children fish from a dock on Sand Lake as the crew of a master boat prepares for an evening practice. The rowing association shares the lake with …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Pennies for Peace ends at Rogers Park Elementary YEARLONG COLLECTION: School raised about $7,500 for charity. By S. Jane Szabo Anchorage Daily News (Published: June 10, 2002) Students participating in the Pennies for Peace program at Rogers Park Elementary School took a final count recently after a year of collecting coppers. Mary Hallman's sixth-grade class scrounged the most spare change for the Resolving Conflicts Creatively Program and was rewarded with a pizza party on the last day of school. RCCP brings peacemaking techniques to the classroom and student mediators to the playground in many local schools. It is supported by a federal grant, and some schools raise funds to enhance the program. Five student mediators from Rogers Park counted the coins at Denali Alaskan Federal Credi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Schools set for summer classes CATCH-UP: Many courses are designed for students who have fallen behind. By Katie Pesznecker Anchorage Daily News (Published: June 4, 2002) School's out Thursday for Anchorage School District students -- or at least, for most of them. About 3,400 kids -- roughly 7 percent of the district's 49,000 students -- are expected to enroll in summer school. This year's program includes traditional academic fare that students must master to pass state-required tests: intense remedial courses in reading, writing and math. As the focus on state-required tests increases, so does pressure -- on schools and parents, as well as students -- to keep all kids on track. Students in grades three through nine take state benchmark tests and national standardized tests. Those in…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Inside Alaska Business (Published: June 28, 2002) Seafood company taps top execs Alaska Seafood International, the South Anchorage fish processing plant marketing goods under the trade name Great Alaskan Seafood Co., has named two top managers: Philip Fitzpatrick, a former Bumble Bee Seafoods executive, and Jeffrey Hester, already on the company's board of directors. They will serve in the office of the president along with Russell Schreck, Great Alaskan's chief executive. The company's majority owner is Sunrise Capital Partners of New York City. The state also owns an interest and the factory building. The plant, recently closed for equipment retooling, will resume production next month, Sunrise managing partner David Preiser said Thursday. Calista signs $1.1 billion contract A Calist…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Woman fired in retaliation, state examiner finds DECISION: Office Products Services says worker was disruptive on the job. By Lisa Demer Anchorage Daily News (Published: June 27, 2002) A saleswoman at an Anchorage copier business complained of sexual discrimination by one of her bosses. Celeste Dorsey wrote to the president of Office Products Services that another co-owner "continually degraded, embarrassed or abused" her and that she wouldn't tolerate it. Two days later, she was fired. Now, a state hearing examiner has found that Dorsey was illegally retaliated against. The company should be forced to pay her more than $140,000, hearing examiner Robert Landau wrote in his recommended decision, dated June 20. The case for Dorsey was argued by staff with the Alaska State Commission for …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Angry parents hope for federal attention REVIEW: Agency undergoing audit now; findings due soon. By Lisa Demer Anchorage Daily News (Published: June 28, 2002) Zaire Amin, Left, And Division Of Family And Youth Services Director Theresa Tanoury Meet During A Protest Downtown. (Photo by Jim Lavrakas / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Alice Brown says she held her baby daughter just once before the state Division of Family and Youth Services took her away. The state feared for the baby's safety after a difficult, unplanned pregnancy, though Brown denies making any threatening statements. So before Jessica Lani Cynnamon Brown was a day old, she joined more than 1,800 children in state custody. "I was in utter shock. What is going on here? How can they take my baby at birth? …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Anchorage adults get a taste of life in the Bush TATITLEK: Residents took part in village's heritage week. By Asta Corley Anchorage Daily News (Published: June 19, 2002) A couple of Anchorage residents recently immersed themselves in rural Alaska life during Cultural Heritage Week in Tatitlek. Matt Rafferty and Robin Kornfield traveled there in early May as part of the pilot project for the Urban-Rural Exchange for Adults. The project serves as an extension of Leadership Anchorage, a program offered by the Alaska Humanities Forum. It helps foster civic participation from diverse sectors of the community. "Basically, we are all Alaskans, and we need to find common ground and embrace our collective diversity," Rafferty said. He and Kornfield are both members of Leadership Anchorage. Fest…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Local folks (Published: June 21, 2002) The 2002 All-Alaska Bus Roadeo was held at Fort Richardson in May. Winners were Sue Weatherby, Anchorage, 40-foot bus division; Linda Farmer, Fairbanks, 30-foot bus division; Debbie Davis, Anchorage, paratransit division; and Michael Wilson, Wasilla, vanpool division. Carol M. White, Sen. Ted Stevens' chief of staff and longtime aide, was awarded the Sen. Ted Stevens Award by P.E.4 life, a nonprofit organization that strives to promote quality, daily physical education among America's youths. Pat Hjellen of Wasilla, a member of Susitna Auxiliary No. 9365, joined other key leaders of the Ladies Auxiliary to the Veterans of Foreign Wars of the United States at its 2002 National Mid-Year Conference in San Juan, Puerto Rico. Emily Evans of Anchorage m…

summary: In Alaska Business News Winter gains By Sarana Schell Anchorage Daily News (Published: July 3, 2002) Besides an intangible boost to community spirit, the 2001 Special Olympics World Winter Games brought the Anchorage Bowl some very tangible rewards. A study by the University of Alaska Anchorage's Institute of Social and Economic Research said the 2001 Special Olympics World Winter Games pumped $22 million into the economy of the Anchorage Bowl. Vida Flis of Slovenia, above, tossed her hat into the air in celebration shortly before receiving a medal for cross-country skiing.
